title: The odd-parity ALM induced reconstruction of the Chern-insulating phase in Haldane-Hubbard model
authors: Minghuan Zeng, Zheng Qin, Ling Qin, Shiping Feng, Lin Wu, Dong-Hui Xu, Rui Wang
abstract: Odd-parity altermagnetism(ALM) extends compensated collinear magnetism beyond the even-parity spin splitting of conventional altermagnets, but its role in correlated topological phases remains largely unexplored. Using the cluster slave-spin method, we show that the odd-parity ALM appearing in the ALM Chern-insulating phase of Haldane-Hubbard model significantly reconstructs the local topology in the conventional Chern-insulating phase, while the total Chern number remains unchanged compared to the Chern-insulating phase. The Berry curvature becomes spin and valley selective; zigzag ribbons develop chiral-symmetry-breaking edge states; while armchair ribbons remain inversion symmetric. The optical response mirrors this separation between the local reconstruction and the global topology: low-energy spectra are governed by quasiparticles near the gap, whereas the low-frequency Hall conductivity stays quantized, $σ_{\rm T\uparrow}(Ω\to 0)=σ_{\rm T\downarrow}(Ω\to 0)=e^2/h$. These results establish the Haldane-Hubbard model as a minimal correlated platform for odd-parity altermagnetic topology.
summary: This paper explores how odd-parity altermagnetism affects the topological properties of the Haldane-Hubbard model. It demonstrates that while the global Chern number remains invariant during the transition to an altermagnetic Chern-insulating phase, the local Berry curvature is significantly redistributed. This reconstruction leads to spin-valley-locked edge states in zigzag ribbons and specific signatures in the optical Hall response. The findings establish the Haldane-Hubbard model as a key platform for studying the interplay of symmetry, interactions, and topology.
main_problem: The study investigates how odd-parity altermagnetism (ALM) reconstructs the momentum-space topology, edge states, and Hall transport in the presence of electronic interactions in a Chern insulator.
main_result: The transition from a conventional Chern-insulating phase to an ALM-induced phase redistributes Berry curvature and alters edge state symmetry without changing the total Chern number, while maintaining quantized low-frequency Hall conductivity.
method: The authors employ the cluster slave-spin method, utilizing a 2-site cluster with exact diagonalization and a mean-field decoupling approach to treat strong electronic correlations.
model_or_system: The Haldane-Hubbard model on a hexagonal lattice, which includes nearest-neighbor hopping, next-nearest-neighbor Haldane hopping, and onsite Coulomb repulsion.
key_observables: Berry curvature, edge state spectra (zigzag and armchair ribbons), Chern number, and optical conductivity (longitudinal and transverse).
important_parameters: Hubbard interaction strength (U), Haldane hopping (lambda), and chemical potential (mu).
main_assumptions: The use of a 2-site cluster slave-spin approximation and a mean-field treatment for the coupling between the cluster and its environment.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the HH model ground state and the slave-spin approximation scheme; Fig 2 presents the phase diagram and spinon energy dispersion; Fig 3 illustrates spin-resolved Berry curvature across different interaction regimes; Fig 4 details the evolution of zigzag edge spectra and LDOS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of odd-parity ALM in correlated phases, describes the HH model and slave-spin method, presents the phase diagram and Berry curvature redistribution, analyzes edge state evolution in different ribbon geometries, discusses optical response, and concludes with experimental implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(Color online) (a)The schematic illustration of the ground state of hexagon-lattice HH model having the compensated collinear magnetism with opposite sublattice currents denoted by slight blue arrows, where the bold up-(red) and downward(blue) solid arrows represent the corresponding spin polarizations. (b)The schematic illustration of the 2-site cluster slave spin approximation, where the NN hopping denoted by the blue solid line and the onsite electron Coulomb repulsion within the 2-site cluster are treated using the numerical method of exact diagonalization, while the coupling between this 2-site cluster and the environment, denoted by the red and blue dashed lines, are roughly...

FIG. 2. (Color online) (a)The phase diagram of HH model as a function of Haldane hopping λ and onsite electron Coulomb repulsion U, which consists of four regimes: AFMI at λ = 0 for U > UAFM ≈3.1, CI with the Thouless-Kohmoto- Nightingale-Nijs number C = 2 at small U ′s, the odd-parity ALM-CI phase with C = 2 and |M| > 0 in the intermediate interaction regime, and odd-parity ALMI at large interaction strengths[From Ref. 19]. (b)The momentum dependence of the spinon energy dispersion Ekσ in Eq.(9) along the zigzag direction in the first Brillouin zone at λ = 0.3 as well as U =4.8(black dashed) and 5.5 for up(red solid) and down(black solid) spinons. FIG. 4. (Color online)The zigzag energy dispersion as a function of momentum ky mapped by the electron spectrum function Aσ(ky, ω) at λ = 0.3 with (a)U = 4.8(CI) and (b)5.5(ALM-CI), respectively. (c)the electronic LDOS of the edge mode as a function of site location j in the CI state with λ = 0.3 and U = 4.8; (d)the up- and (e)down-spin electronic LDOS of the edge mode in the ALM-CI phase with λ = 0.3 and U = 5.5. Here, the the color scale at the right- hand side in the first row measures the spectral weight for up-spin electrons; the legend ”left” and ”right” in subfigure (c) represent the left and right edge mode in the first row, respectively.

FIG. 5. (Color online)The armchair energy dispersion as a function of momentum kx mapped by the electron spectrum function Aσ(kx, ω) at λ = 0.3 with (a)U = 4.8(CI) and (b)5.5(ALM-CI). The corresponding electron LDOS of the edge modes as a function of site location j are respectively plotted in subfigure (c) and (d). Here, the legend ”left” and ”right” represent the left and right edge mode in the first row, respectively.
